**Shinjuku** ([Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language"): 新宿区, [Hepburn](/wiki/Hepburn_romanization "Hepburn romanization"): *Shinjuku-ku*; IPA: [[ɕiɲdʑɯkɯ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Japanese "Help:IPA/Japanese") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:TomJ-Shinjuku.ogg "File:TomJ-Shinjuku.ogg")), officially called **Shinjuku City**, is one of the 23 [special wards](/wiki/Special_wards_of_Tokyo "Special wards of Tokyo") of [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo").

Since the end of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), Shinjuku has become a major secondary center of Tokyo (*[fukutoshin](/wiki/Tokyo_Metro_Fukutoshin_Line#History "Tokyo Metro Fukutoshin Line")*), rivaling the original city center in [Marunouchi](/wiki/Marunouchi "Marunouchi"). Today, it is a major commercial and administrative center, housing the busiest railway station in the world ([Shinjuku Station](/wiki/Shinjuku_Station "Shinjuku Station")) as well as the [Tokyo Metropolitan Government Building](/wiki/Tokyo_Metropolitan_Government_Building "Tokyo Metropolitan Government Building"), the administrative center of the [Tokyo Metropolitan Government](/wiki/Tokyo_Metropolitan_Government "Tokyo Metropolitan Government").

As of 2025, the ward has an estimated population of 355,981,[[4]](#cite_note-4) living in a total area of 18.23 km2.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shinjuku&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Buildings_with_colorful_neon_street_signs_at_blue_hour%2C_Shinjuku%2C_Tokyo.jpg/330px-Buildings_with_colorful_neon_street_signs_at_blue_hour%2C_Shinjuku%2C_Tokyo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Buildings_with_colorful_neon_street_signs_at_blue_hour,_Shinjuku,_Tokyo.jpg)

Shinjuku at night

In 1634, during the [Edo period](/wiki/Edo_period "Edo period"), as the outer moat of the [Edo Castle](/wiki/Edo_Castle "Edo Castle") was built, a number of temples and shrines moved to the Yotsuya area on the western edge of Shinjuku. In 1698, Naitō-Shinjuku had developed as a new (shin) station ([shuku or juku](/wiki/Shukuba "Shukuba")) on the [Kōshū Kaidō](/wiki/K%C5%8Dsh%C5%AB_Kaid%C5%8D "Kōshū Kaidō"), one of the major [highways](/wiki/Kaid%C5%8D "Kaidō") of that era. Naitō was the family name of a *[daimyō](/wiki/Daimy%C5%8D "Daimyō")* whose mansion stood in the area; his land is now a public park, the Shinjuku Gyoen. In 1898, the Yodobashi Water Purification Plant, the city's first modern water treatment facility, was built in the area that is now between the park and the train station.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

In 1920, the town of Naitō-Shinjuku, which comprised large parts of present-day Shinjuku (the neighborhood, not the municipality), parts of [Nishi-Shinjuku](/wiki/Nishi-Shinjuku "Nishi-Shinjuku") and [Kabukichō](/wiki/Kabukich%C5%8D,_Tokyo "Kabukichō, Tokyo") were integrated into [Tokyo City](/wiki/Tokyo_City "Tokyo City"). Shinjuku began to develop into its current form after the [Great Kantō Earthquake](/wiki/1923_Great_Kant%C5%8D_earthquake "1923 Great Kantō earthquake") in 1923, since the seismically stable area largely escaped the devastation. Consequently, West Shinjuku is one of the few areas in Tokyo with many [skyscrapers](/wiki/Skyscraper "Skyscraper").

The [Tokyo air raids](/wiki/Bombing_of_Tokyo_in_World_War_II "Bombing of Tokyo in World War II") from May to August 1945 destroyed almost 90% of the buildings in the area in and around Shinjuku Station.[[7]](#cite_note-7) The pre-war form of Shinjuku and the rest of Tokyo was retained after the war because the roads and rails, damaged as they were, remained, and these formed the heart of Shinjuku in the post-war construction. Only in Kabuki-cho was a grand reconstruction plan put into action.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

The present ward was established on March 15, 1947 with the merger of the former wards of Yotsuya, Ushigome, and Yodobashi. It served as part of the [athletics](/wiki/Athletics_at_the_1964_Summer_Olympics "Athletics at the 1964 Summer Olympics") 50 km walk and marathon course during the [1964 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1964_Summer_Olympics "1964 Summer Olympics").[[9]](#cite_note-9) In March 1965, the Yodobashi Water Purification Plant closed and was replaced by skyscrapers in the following years.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

### The Sixties in Shinjuku

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shinjuku&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: The Sixties in Shinjuku")]

Shinjuku was the epicenter of new ideas and artistic avant-gardes in Tokyo in the 1960s, much like [Greenwich Village](/wiki/Greenwich_Village "Greenwich Village") in New York.

#### Cinema

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shinjuku&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Cinema")]

The young directors of the [Japanese New Wave](/wiki/Japanese_New_Wave "Japanese New Wave") used Shinjuku as the setting for several of their films,[[11]](#cite_note-11) such as [Nagisa Oshima](/wiki/Nagisa_%C5%8Cshima "Nagisa Ōshima")'s *[Diary of a Shinjuku Thief](/wiki/Diary_of_a_Shinjuku_Thief "Diary of a Shinjuku Thief")* (1969), [Koji Wakamatsu](/wiki/K%C5%8Dji_Wakamatsu "Kōji Wakamatsu")'s *Shinjuku Mad* (screenplay by [Masao Adachi](/wiki/Masao_Adachi "Masao Adachi")), and *[Funeral Parade of Roses](/wiki/Funeral_Parade_of_Roses "Funeral Parade of Roses")* by [Toshio Matsumoto](/wiki/Toshio_Matsumoto "Toshio Matsumoto").

In 1967, the [Art Theatre Guild](/wiki/Art_Theatre_Guild "Art Theatre Guild") opened the Scorpio Theatre (Sasori-za) in the basement of its [Art Theatre Shinjuku Bunka](/wiki/Art_Theatre_Shinjuku_Bunka "Art Theatre Shinjuku Bunka") cinema. The Scorpio Theatre quickly established itself as an influential underground venue[[12]](#cite_note-12) for [angura](/wiki/Angura "Angura") theater, music, dance, and film. Its name, proposed by novelist [Yukio Mishima](/wiki/Yukio_Mishima "Yukio Mishima"), was inspired by [Kenneth Anger](/wiki/Kenneth_Anger "Kenneth Anger")'s film *[Scorpio Rising](/wiki/Scorpio_Rising_(film) "Scorpio Rising (film)")* (1963). It was at the Sasori-za that Mishima held the first screening of his film *[Patriotism](/wiki/Patriotism "Patriotism")* (1966).

#### Theater

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shinjuku&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Theater")]

In 1967, the Modern Art Theater was opened in Shinjuku. Dubbed the "sensational cave," it offered nude shows, avant-garde and entertainment theater, film screenings, and performances. The theater laboratory "Ceiling Pier", which had [Tadanori Yokoo](/wiki/Tadanori_Yokoo "Tadanori Yokoo") as a founding member, also performed there. After the underground boom, the venue was used for striptease performances.

It was in Shinjuku, in the Hanazono shrine, that the first performances of the "Red Tent" (Jokyo Gekijo), the company founded by [Juro Kara](/wiki/J%C5%ABr%C5%8D_Kara "Jūrō Kara"), took place.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

[Tatsumi Hijikata](/wiki/Tatsumi_Hijikata "Tatsumi Hijikata") and the [buto](/wiki/Butoh "Butoh") dancers were also closely associated to Shinjuku.

#### Places

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shinjuku&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Places")]

Cafe Fugetsudo, founded by actor Goro Yokoyama, was a rallying point for artists, intellectuals, and homosexuals in the 1960s.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Actor [Takeshi Kitano](/wiki/Takeshi_Kitano "Takeshi Kitano"), painter [Taro Okamoto](/wiki/Tar%C5%8D_Okamoto "Tarō Okamoto"), transvestite actress [Akihiro Miwa](/wiki/Akihiro_Miwa "Akihiro Miwa"), poet [Gozo Yoshimasu](/wiki/G%C5%8Dz%C5%8D_Yoshimasu "Gōzō Yoshimasu"), and filmmaker [Shuji Terayama](/wiki/Sh%C5%ABji_Terayama "Shūji Terayama") frequented it.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

In the [Golden Gai](/wiki/Shinjuku_Golden_Gai "Shinjuku Golden Gai"), a small block located east of [Kabukicho](/wiki/Kabukich%C5%8D "Kabukichō"), many bars have been haunts of artists and protesters.

#### Folk Guerilla Concerts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shinjuku&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Folk Guerilla Concerts")]

In 1969, anti-war group [Beheiren](/wiki/Beheiren "Beheiren") organized folk concerts in Shinjuku station. A batch of Japanese anti-Vietnam War activists gathered and were termed "[folk guerrillas](/wiki/Folk_guerrilla_concerts "Folk guerrilla concerts")".[[16]](#cite_note-16) In July, a fight with riot police led to the dispersal of the concerts and the arrests of musicians.

#### Demonstrations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shinjuku&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Demonstrations")]

On October 21, 1969, an anti-war demonstration was violently suppressed,[[17]](#cite_note-17) so much so that the daily newspaper *[Asahi](/wiki/The_Asahi_Shimbun "The Asahi Shimbun")* ran the following day's headline: "Guerrilla Warfare in the Heart of Shinjuku."

In 1991, the Tokyo Metropolitan Government moved from the [Marunouchi](/wiki/Marunouchi "Marunouchi") district of [Chiyoda](/wiki/Chiyoda,_Tokyo "Chiyoda, Tokyo") to the current building in Shinjuku (the [Tokyo International Forum](/wiki/Tokyo_International_Forum "Tokyo International Forum") stands at the former site vacated by the government).

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shinjuku&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Shinjuku_large-guard_2023.jpg/250px-Shinjuku_large-guard_2023.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shinjuku_large-guard_2023.jpg)

Shinjuku office buildings

Most of Shinjuku is occupied by the Yodobashi Plateau, the most elevated portion of which extends through most of the Shinjuku Station area. The Kanda River runs through the Ochiai and Totsuka areas near sea level, but the Toshima Plateau also builds elevation in the northern extremities of Totsuka and Ochiai. The highest point in Shinjuku is Hakone-san in Toyama Park, 44.6 m above sea level.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

Shinjuku is surrounded by [Chiyoda](/wiki/Chiyoda,_Tokyo "Chiyoda, Tokyo") to the east; [Bunkyo](/wiki/Bunkyo,_Tokyo "Bunkyo, Tokyo") and [Toshima](/wiki/Toshima,_Tokyo_(ward) "Toshima, Tokyo (ward)") to the north; [Nakano](/wiki/Nakano,_Tokyo "Nakano, Tokyo") to the west, and [Shibuya](/wiki/Shibuya,_Tokyo "Shibuya, Tokyo") and [Minato](/wiki/Minato,_Tokyo "Minato, Tokyo") to the south.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Shinjuku_Gyoen_National_Garden_and_NTT_DoCoMo_Yoyogi_Building%2C_Tokyo%2C_Japan.jpg/250px-Shinjuku_Gyoen_National_Garden_and_NTT_DoCoMo_Yoyogi_Building%2C_Tokyo%2C_Japan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shinjuku_Gyoen_National_Garden_and_NTT_DoCoMo_Yoyogi_Building,_Tokyo,_Japan.jpg)

[Shinjuku Gyo-en National Garden](/wiki/Shinjuku_Gyo-en "Shinjuku Gyo-en") and [NTT DoCoMo Yoyogi Building](/wiki/NTT_Docomo_Yoyogi_Building "NTT Docomo Yoyogi Building")

The current city of Shinjuku grew out of several separate towns and villages, which have retained some distinctions despite growing together as part of the Tokyo metropolis.

* East Shinjuku : the area east of [Shinjuku Station](/wiki/Shinjuku_Station "Shinjuku Station") between [Higashi-Shinjuku Station](/wiki/Higashi-Shinjuku_Station "Higashi-Shinjuku Station") and [Shinjuku-sanchome Station](/wiki/Shinjuku-sanchome_Station "Shinjuku-sanchome Station"), historically known as Naito-Shinjuku, houses the city hall and the flagship [Isetan](/wiki/Isetan "Isetan") department store, as well as several famous districts :
  + [Kabukichō](/wiki/Kabukich%C5%8D,_Tokyo "Kabukichō, Tokyo"): the red-light district, renowned for its variety of bars, restaurants, and [sex-related establishments](/wiki/Sex_trade "Sex trade") near [Seibu-Shinjuku Station](/wiki/Seibu-Shinjuku_Station "Seibu-Shinjuku Station").
  + [Golden Gai](/wiki/Golden_Gai "Golden Gai"): an area of tiny shanty-style bars and clubs, where musicians, artists, journalists, actors and directors used to gather.
  + [Shinjuku Gyo-en](/wiki/Shinjuku_Gyo-en "Shinjuku Gyo-en"): A large park, 58.3 hectares, blending [Japanese traditional](/wiki/Japanese_garden "Japanese garden"), [English landscape](/wiki/Landscape_garden "Landscape garden") and [French formal](/wiki/French_garden "French garden") style gardens.
  + [Shinjuku Ni-chōme](/wiki/Shinjuku_Ni-ch%C5%8Dme "Shinjuku Ni-chōme"): Tokyo's best-known [gay](/wiki/Gay "Gay") district.
* [Nishi-Shinjuku](/wiki/Nishi-Shinjuku "Nishi-Shinjuku"): The area west of Shinjuku Station, historically known as Yodobashi, is home to Tokyo's largest concentration of [skyscrapers](/wiki/Skyscrapers "Skyscrapers"). Several of the [tallest buildings in Tokyo](/wiki/Tallest_buildings_in_Tokyo "Tallest buildings in Tokyo") are located in this area, including the Tokyo Metropolitan Government Building, KDDI Building and [Park Tower](/wiki/Shinjuku_Park_Tower "Shinjuku Park Tower").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/%E6%96%B0%E5%AE%BF%E5%8C%BA%E4%B8%8B%E8%90%BD%E5%90%88_%E6%96%B0%E7%9B%AE%E7%99%BD%E9%80%9A%E3%82%8A_20250928.jpg/250px-%E6%96%B0%E5%AE%BF%E5%8C%BA%E4%B8%8B%E8%90%BD%E5%90%88_%E6%96%B0%E7%9B%AE%E7%99%BD%E9%80%9A%E3%82%8A_20250928.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E6%96%B0%E5%AE%BF%E5%8C%BA%E4%B8%8B%E8%90%BD%E5%90%88_%E6%96%B0%E7%9B%AE%E7%99%BD%E9%80%9A%E3%82%8A_20250928.jpg)

Ochiai area Shinmejiro St.（2025）

* Ochiai: The northwestern corner of Shinjuku, extending to the area around [Ochiai-minami-nagasaki Station](/wiki/Ochiai-minami-nagasaki_Station "Ochiai-minami-nagasaki Station") and the south side of [Mejiro Station](/wiki/Mejiro_Station "Mejiro Station"), is largely residential with a small business district around [Nakai Station](/wiki/Nakai_Station "Nakai Station").
* [Ōkubo](/wiki/%C5%8Ckubo,_Tokyo "Ōkubo, Tokyo"): The area surrounding [Okubo](/wiki/%C5%8Ckubo_Station_(Tokyo) "Ōkubo Station (Tokyo)") and [Shin-Okubo Station](/wiki/Shin-Okubo_Station "Shin-Okubo Station") is best known as Tokyo's historic ethnic Korean neighborhood.
* [Shinanomachi](/wiki/Shinanomachi "Shinanomachi") : A district reputed to be the headquarters of a Buddhist organization, which owns many buildings there.
* Totsuka: The northern portion of Shinjuku surrounding [Takadanobaba Station](/wiki/Takadanobaba_Station "Takadanobaba Station") and [Waseda University](/wiki/Waseda_University "Waseda University"), today commonly referred to as Nishi-Waseda. The [Takadanobaba](/wiki/Takadanobaba "Takadanobaba") area is a major residential and nightlife area for students, as well as a commuter hub.
* [Toyama](/wiki/Toyama,_Shinjuku "Toyama, Shinjuku"): A largely residential and school area, in the east of Ōkubo and south of [Waseda University](/wiki/Waseda_University "Waseda University"), extending to the area around [Nishi-Waseda Station](/wiki/Nishi-Waseda_Station "Nishi-Waseda Station"), [Gakushuin Women's College](/wiki/Gakushuin_Women%27s_College "Gakushuin Women's College") and Toyama Park.
* [Ushigome](/wiki/Ushigome,_Tokyo "Ushigome, Tokyo"): A largely residential area in the eastern portion of the city.
  + [Ichigaya](/wiki/Ichigaya "Ichigaya"): A commercial area in eastern Shinjuku, site of the [Ministry of Defense](/wiki/Ministry_of_Defense_(Japan) "Ministry of Defense (Japan)").
  + [Kagurazaka](/wiki/Kagurazaka "Kagurazaka"): A hill descending to the [Iidabashi Station](/wiki/Iidabashi_Station "Iidabashi Station") area, once one of Tokyo's last remaining *[hanamachi](/wiki/Hanamachi "Hanamachi")* or *[geisha](/wiki/Geisha "Geisha")* districts, and currently known for hosting a sizable French community.[[20]](#cite_note-20)
* [Yotsuya](/wiki/Yotsuya "Yotsuya"): An upscale residential and commercial district in the southeast corner of Shinjuku. The Arakichō area is well known for its many small restaurants, bars, and *izakaya*.

"Shinjuku" is often popularly understood to mean the entire area surrounding [Shinjuku Station](/wiki/Shinjuku_Station "Shinjuku Station"), but the Shinjuku Southern Terrace complex and the areas to the west of the station and south of [Kōshū Kaidō](/wiki/K%C5%8Dsh%C5%AB_Kaid%C5%8D "Kōshū Kaidō") are part of the [Yoyogi](/wiki/Yoyogi "Yoyogi") and [Sendagaya](/wiki/Sendagaya "Sendagaya") districts of the special ward of [Shibuya](/wiki/Shibuya,_Tokyo "Shibuya, Tokyo").

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shinjuku&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Economy")]

[![Buildings with colorful neon street signs at blue hour, Shinjuku, Tokyo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Buildings_with_colorful_neon_street_signs_at_blue_hour%2C_Shinjuku%2C_Tokyo.jpg/250px-Buildings_with_colorful_neon_street_signs_at_blue_hour%2C_Shinjuku%2C_Tokyo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Buildings_with_colorful_neon_street_signs_at_blue_hour,_Shinjuku,_Tokyo.jpg)

Shinjuku's entertainment district

The area surrounding Shinjuku Station is a major economic hub of Tokyo. Many companies have their headquarters or Tokyo offices in this area, including regional telephone operator [NTT East](/wiki/Nippon_Telegraph_and_Telephone "Nippon Telegraph and Telephone"), global camera and medical device manufacturer [Olympus Corporation](/wiki/Olympus_Corporation "Olympus Corporation"), electronics giant [Seiko Epson](/wiki/Seiko_Epson "Seiko Epson"),[[21]](#cite_note-21) video game developer [Square Enix](/wiki/Square_Enix "Square Enix"),[[22]](#cite_note-22) fast food chains [McDonald's Japan](/wiki/McDonald%27s_Japan "McDonald's Japan") and [Yoshinoya](/wiki/Yoshinoya "Yoshinoya"),[[23]](#cite_note-23) travel agency [H.I.S.](/wiki/H.I.S._(travel_agency) "H.I.S. (travel agency)"),[[24]](#cite_note-24) [Subaru Corporation](/wiki/Subaru_Corporation "Subaru Corporation") ([Subaru](/wiki/Subaru "Subaru")),[[25]](#cite_note-25) railway operator [Odakyu Electric Railway](/wiki/Odakyu_Electric_Railway "Odakyu Electric Railway"), construction giants [Taisei Corporation](/wiki/Taisei_Corporation "Taisei Corporation")[[26]](#cite_note-26) and [Kumagai Gumi](/wiki/Kumagai_Gumi "Kumagai Gumi"),[[27]](#cite_note-27) medical equipment manufacturer [Nihon Kohden](/wiki/Nihon_Kohden "Nihon Kohden"),[[28]](#cite_note-28) [Enoki Films](/wiki/Enoki_Films "Enoki Films"),[[29]](#cite_note-29) navigation software company [Jorudan](/wiki/Jorudan "Jorudan"),[[30]](#cite_note-30) instant noodle giant [Nissin Foods](/wiki/Nissin_Foods "Nissin Foods"),[[31]](#cite_note-31) automotive components manufacturer [Keihin Corporation](/wiki/Keihin_Corporation "Keihin Corporation"),[[32]](#cite_note-32) and regional airline [Airtransse](/wiki/Airtransse "Airtransse").[[33]](#cite_note-33) The station area also hosts numerous major retailers such as [Isetan](/wiki/Isetan "Isetan"), [Takashimaya](/wiki/Takashimaya "Takashimaya"), [Marui](/wiki/Marui "Marui"), [Bic Camera](/wiki/Bic_Camera "Bic Camera"), [Yodobashi Camera](/wiki/Yodobashi_Camera "Yodobashi Camera") and [Yamada Denki](/wiki/Yamada_Denki "Yamada Denki").

Northeastern Shinjuku has an active publishing industry and is home to the publishers [Shinchosha](/wiki/Shinchosha "Shinchosha")[[34]](#cite_note-34) and [Futabasha](/wiki/Futabasha "Futabasha").[[35]](#cite_note-35) The main store of the [Books Kinokuniya](/wiki/Books_Kinokuniya "Books Kinokuniya") bookstore chain is also located in Shinjuku.

A giant 3D cat projected near the east exit of Shinjuku Station

Like the other [special wards of Tokyo](/wiki/Special_wards_of_Tokyo "Special wards of Tokyo"), Shinjuku has a status equivalent to that of a [city](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Japan "Municipalities of Japan"). The current mayor is Kenichi Yoshizumi.

The ward council (区議会, *kugikai*) consists of 38 elected members; the [Liberal Democratic Party](/wiki/Liberal_Democratic_Party_(Japan) "Liberal Democratic Party (Japan)") and [New Komeitō Party](/wiki/New_Komeit%C5%8D_Party "New Komeitō Party") together currently hold a majority. The [Democratic Party of Japan](/wiki/Democratic_Party_of_Japan "Democratic Party of Japan"), [Japanese Communist Party](/wiki/Japanese_Communist_Party "Japanese Communist Party") and the [Social Democratic Party](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_(Japan) "Social Democratic Party (Japan)") are also represented together with four [independents](/wiki/Independents_(politician) "Independents (politician)"). Shinjuku's city office (区役所, *kuyakusho*) is located on the southeastern edge of Kabukichō.

Shinjuku is also the location of the [Tokyo Metropolitan Government](/wiki/Tokyo_Metropolitan_Government "Tokyo Metropolitan Government"). The governor's office, the metropolitan assembly chamber, and all administrative head offices are located in the [Tokyo Metropolitan Government Building](/wiki/Tokyo_Metropolitan_Government_Building "Tokyo Metropolitan Government Building").

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shinjuku&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Transportation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Shinjuku_busker.jpg/250px-Shinjuku_busker.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shinjuku_busker.jpg)

A guitarist immediately south of the Shinjuku JR Station, a popular [busking](/wiki/Busking "Busking") location

Further information: [Transport in Greater Tokyo](/wiki/Transport_in_Greater_Tokyo "Transport in Greater Tokyo")

Shinjuku is a major urban transit hub. [Shinjuku Station](/wiki/Shinjuku_Station "Shinjuku Station") sees an estimated 3.64 million passengers pass through each day, making it the busiest station in the world. It houses interchanges to three subway lines and three privately owned commuter lines, as well as several JR lines.

The southern half of this area surrounding the station and majority of the station are in fact located in the neighboring [Shibuya](/wiki/Shibuya "Shibuya") ward.
